title: Economy Basics O/X
description: Core ideas about money and the economy

cards.prompt: Inflation refers to a general rise in prices over time.
cards.explanation: Inflation means the overall level of prices keeps rising.

cards.prompt: When demand rises and supply stays the same, prices usually fall.
cards.explanation: When demand rises with fixed supply, prices usually rise, not fall.

cards.prompt: A central bank can influence interest rates in an economy.
cards.explanation: Central banks set policy rates that guide interest rates across the economy.

cards.prompt: GDP stands for the total population of a country.
cards.explanation: GDP means Gross Domestic Product, the value of goods and services produced.

cards.prompt: Diversifying investments can help reduce risk.
cards.explanation: Spreading money across assets lowers the impact of any single loss.

cards.prompt: A budget deficit means income is greater than spending.
cards.explanation: A deficit means spending is greater than income.

cards.prompt: Banks accept deposits and provide loans.
cards.explanation: Banks take in deposits and lend money out to borrowers.

cards.prompt: Buying a company's stock makes you a creditor who lent it money.
cards.explanation: Buying stock makes you a shareholder who owns part of the company.

cards.prompt: Compound interest means earning interest on both principal and prior interest.
cards.explanation: With compound interest, earned interest is added and itself earns interest.

cards.prompt: Deflation means prices are continuously rising.
cards.explanation: Deflation means prices are continuously falling; rising prices are inflation.

cards.prompt: Supply and demand influence the price of goods.
cards.explanation: The balance of supply and demand shapes market prices.

cards.prompt: Taxes are voluntary donations that citizens may choose not to pay.
cards.explanation: Taxes are compulsory payments required by law, not voluntary donations.

cards.prompt: A recession is a significant, broad decline in economic activity.
cards.explanation: A recession is a widespread drop in output, income, and employment lasting more than a few months.

cards.prompt: When a country's currency gains value, its imports become more expensive.
cards.explanation: A stronger currency buys more foreign goods, so imports actually become cheaper.

cards.prompt: Interest is the cost of borrowing money.
cards.explanation: Borrowers pay interest as the price of using a lender's money over time.

cards.prompt: A bond represents a share of ownership in a company.
cards.explanation: A bond is a loan to the issuer; stocks, not bonds, represent ownership.

cards.prompt: The unemployment rate measures the share of the labor force that is jobless but seeking work.
cards.explanation: It counts people who are actively looking for a job but do not have one, as a share of the labor force.

cards.prompt: Printing more money always makes a country wealthier.
cards.explanation: Printing money without more goods and services tends to cause inflation, not real wealth.

cards.prompt: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best option you give up when making a choice.
cards.explanation: Every choice means forgoing alternatives; the best forgone alternative is the opportunity cost.

cards.prompt: The stock market only ever moves upward and never falls.
cards.explanation: Stock prices rise and fall; markets regularly experience declines as well as gains.

cards.prompt: A monopoly is a market controlled by a single seller.
cards.explanation: In a monopoly, one seller dominates the market with no close competitors.

cards.prompt: Money you deposit at a bank sits locked in a vault and is never lent out.
cards.explanation: Banks keep only part of deposits on hand and lend the rest out to earn interest.

cards.prompt: Exports are goods and services sold to other countries.
cards.explanation: Exports are what a country sells abroad, while imports are what it buys from abroad.

cards.prompt: A higher credit score generally makes it more expensive to borrow money.
cards.explanation: A higher credit score usually earns lower interest rates, making borrowing cheaper.

cards.prompt: Fiscal policy involves government decisions on spending and taxation.
cards.explanation: Fiscal policy uses government spending and taxes to influence the economy.

cards.prompt: Barter requires the use of money to trade goods.
cards.explanation: Barter is the direct exchange of goods or services without using money.

cards.prompt: Scarcity means resources are limited relative to people's wants.
cards.explanation: Scarcity is the basic economic problem that unlimited wants exceed limited resources.

cards.prompt: Inflation increases the purchasing power of money.
cards.explanation: Inflation raises prices, so each unit of money buys less; its purchasing power falls.

cards.prompt: A dividend is a payment made to shareholders out of a company's profits.
cards.explanation: Companies may distribute part of their earnings to shareholders as dividends.

cards.prompt: Gross profit is calculated after subtracting all of a company's operating expenses.
cards.explanation: Gross profit subtracts only the cost of goods sold; net profit accounts for all expenses.

cards.prompt: A supply chain links producers of raw materials through to final consumers.
cards.explanation: A supply chain is the network of steps that turns raw materials into products delivered to customers.

cards.prompt: A progressive tax charges lower earners a higher percentage than higher earners.
cards.explanation: A progressive tax charges higher earners a higher percentage, not lower earners.

cards.prompt: Insurance spreads financial risk across many policyholders.
cards.explanation: Many people pay premiums so that the few who suffer losses can be compensated, pooling risk.

cards.prompt: The law of demand says people buy more of a good as its price rises.
cards.explanation: The law of demand states that, all else equal, people buy less as the price rises.

cards.prompt: Capital refers to assets used to produce goods and services.
cards.explanation: Capital includes tools, machines, and buildings used in producing goods and services.

cards.prompt: A trade surplus means a country imports more than it exports.
cards.explanation: A trade surplus means exports exceed imports; the opposite is a trade deficit.
